 99→    }
100→
101→    /**
102→     * Constructs a [LocalDateTime] instance from the given date and time components.
103→     *
104→     * The components [monthNumber] and [dayOfMonth] are 1-based.
105→     *
106→     * The supported ranges of components:
107→     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
108→     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
109→     * - [monthNumber] `1..12`
110→     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
111→     * - [hour] `0..23`
112→     * - [minute] `0..59`
113→     * - [second] `0..59`
114→     * - [nanosecond] `0..999_999_999`
115→     *
116→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the given [monthNumber] and
117→     * [year].
118→     */

120→        year: Int,

122→        dayOfMonth: Int,
123→        hour: Int,
124→        minute: Int,
125→        second: Int = 0,
126→        nanosecond: Int = 0
127→    )
128→
129→    /**

131→     *
132→     * The supported ranges of components:

134→     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
135→     * - [month] all values of the [Month] enum
136→     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
137→     * - [hour] `0..23`
138→     * - [minute] `0..59`
139→     * - [second] `0..59`
140→     * - [nanosecond] `0..999_999_999`
141→     *
142→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the given [month] and
143→     * [year].
144→     */
145→    public constructor(
146→        year: Int,
147→        month: Month,
148→        dayOfMonth: Int,
149→        hour: Int,
150→        minute: Int,

152→        nanosecond: Int = 0
153→    )
154→
155→    /**
156→     * Constructs a [LocalDateTime] instance by combining the given [date] and [time] parts.
157→     */
158→    public constructor(date: LocalDate, time: LocalTime)
159→
160→    /** Returns the year component of the date. */
161→    public val year: Int
162→
163→    /** Returns the number-of-month (1..12) component of the date. */
164→    public val monthNumber: Int
165→
166→    /** Returns the month ([Month]) component of the date. */
167→    public val month: Month
168→
169→    /** Returns the day-of-month component of the date. */
170→    public val dayOfMonth: Int
171→
172→    /** Returns the day-of-week component of the date. */
173→    public val dayOfWeek: DayOfWeek
174→
175→    /** Returns the day-of-year component of the date. */
176→    public val dayOfYear: Int
177→
178→    /** Returns the hour-of-day time component of this date/time value. */
179→    public val hour: Int
180→
181→    /** Returns the minute-of-hour time component of this date/time value. */
182→    public val minute: Int
183→
184→    /** Returns the second-of-minute time component of this date/time value. */
185→    public val second: Int
186→
187→    /** Returns the nanosecond-of-second time component of this date/time value. */
188→    public val nanosecond: Int
189→
190→    /** Returns the date part of this date/time value. */
191→    public val date: LocalDate
192→
193→    /** Returns the time part of this date/time value. */
194→    public val time: LocalTime
195→
196→    /**
197→     * Compares `this` date/time value with the [other] date/time value.
198→     * Returns zero if this value is equal to the other,
199→     * a negative number if this value represents earlier civil time than the other,
200→     * and a positive number if this value represents later civil time than the other.
201→     */
202→    // TODO: add a note about pitfalls of comparing localdatetimes falling in the Autumn transition
203→    public override operator fun compareTo(other: LocalDateTime): Int
204→
205→    /**
206→     * Converts this date/time value to the ISO 8601 string representation.
207→     *
208→     * For readability, if the time represents a round minute (without seconds or fractional seconds),
209→     * the string representation will not include seconds. Also, fractions of seconds will add trailing zeros to
210→     * the fractional part until its length is a multiple of three.
211→     *
212→     * Examples of output:
213→     * - `2020-08-30T18:43`
214→     * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00`
215→     * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00.500`
216→     * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00.123456789`
217→     *
218→     * @see LocalTime.toString for details of how the time part is formatted.
219→     * @see Formats.ISO for a very similar format. The difference is that [Formats.ISO] will always include seconds,
220→     * even if they are zero, and will not add trailing zeros to the fractional part of the second for readability.
221→     * @see parse for the dual operation: obtaining [LocalDateTime] from a string.
222→     * @see LocalDateTime.format for formatting using a custom format.
223→     */
224→    public override fun toString(): String
225→}

230→ */
231→public fun LocalDateTime.format(format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime>): String = format.format(this)
232→
233→/**
234→ * @suppress

236→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("LocalDateTime.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
237→public fun String.toLocalDateTime(): LocalDateTime = LocalDateTime.parse(this)

174→     * "greater" one.
175→     */
176→    public override operator fun compareTo(other: LocalTime): Int
177→
178→    /**
179→     * Converts this time value to the extended ISO-8601 string representation.
180→     *
181→     * For readability, if the time represents a round minute (without seconds or fractional seconds),
182→     * the string representation will not include seconds. Also, fractions of seconds will add trailing zeros to
183→     * the fractional part until the number of digits after the dot is a multiple of three.
184→     *
185→     * Examples of output:
186→     * - `18:43`
187→     * - `18:43:00`
188→     * - `18:43:00.500`
189→     * - `18:43:00.123456789`
190→     *
191→     * @see Formats.ISO for a very similar format. The difference is that [Formats.ISO] will always include seconds,
192→     * even if they are zero, and will not add trailing zeros to the fractional part of the second for readability.
193→     * @see parse for the dual operation: obtaining [LocalTime] from a string.
194→     * @see LocalTime.format for formatting using a custom format.
195→     */
196→    public override fun toString(): String
197→}
198→

201→ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] with `this`.
202→ */
203→public fun LocalTime.format(format: DateTimeFormat<LocalTime>): String = format.format(this)
204→

211→/**

213→ */
214→public fun LocalTime.atDate(year: Int, monthNumber: Int, dayOfMonth: Int = 0): LocalDateTime =

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/LocalTime.kt","old_string":"/**
 * The time part of [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * This class represents time-of-day without a referencing a specific date.
 * To reconstruct a full [LocalDateTime], representing civil date and time, [LocalTime] needs to be
 * combined with [LocalDate] via [LocalDate.atTime] or [LocalTime.atDate].
 *
 * Also, [LocalTime] does not reference a particular time zone.
 * Therefore, even on the same date, [LocalTime] denotes different moments of time.
 * For example, `18:43` happens at different moments in Berlin and in Tokyo.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for the time zone
 * transitions it may give misleading results.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A time-of-day in the ISO-8601 calendar system, such as `18:43:13`.
 *
 * `LocalTime` represents time-of-day (hour, minute, second, and nanosecond) without a reference to a
 * particular date or time zone. As such, it does not denote a specific moment in time.
 * For example, `18:43` happens at different moments in different time zones.
 *
 * ### Acquisition
 * Some ways to acquire a [LocalTime]:
 * - From components using the constructor: `LocalTime(18, 43, 13)`.
 * - By parsing an ISO-8601 string: `LocalTime.parse("18:43:13")`.
 * - From an [Instant]: `instant.toLocalDateTime(timeZone).time`.
 *
 * ### Usage
 * Use `LocalTime` to represent the time of an event that does not have a specific date associated with it,
 * such as a daily alarm.
 *
 * ```kotlin
 * val alarmTime = LocalTime(7, 0, 0)
 * val now = Clock.System.now().toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).time
 * if (now >= alarmTime) {
 *     println("Time to wake up!")
 * }
 * ```
 *
 * To reconstruct a full [LocalDateTime], combine [LocalTime] with [LocalDate] via [LocalDate.atTime] or
 * [LocalTime.atDate].
 *
 * Arithmetic on `LocalTime` is intentionally omitted because it is ill-defined due to daylight saving
 * time transitions.
 *
 * ### Interactions with Native Types
 * On the JVM, [LocalTime] can be converted to and from `java.time.LocalTime` using `toJavaLocalTime()` and
 * `toKotlinLocalTime()` extension functions.
 */"}

 44→         *
 45→         * The supported variants of time zone identifiers:

 47→         * - a string starting with '+', '-', `UTC+`, `UTC-`, `UT+`, `UT-`, `GMT+`, `GMT-` — identifiers the time zone
 48→         *   with the fixed offset specified after `+` or `-`,
 49→         * - all other strings are treated as region-based zone identifiers.
 50→         * In the IANA Time Zone Database (TZDB) which is used as the default source of time zones,
 51→         * these ids are usually in the form `area/city`, for example, `Europe/Berlin` or `America/Los_Angeles`.

 68→     * is not necessary so.

130→public expect fun Instant.toLocalDateTime(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDateTime

134→ *

151→ *

153→ * - There's only one instant that has this date/time value in the [timeZone]. In this case
154→ * the conversion is unambiguous.
155→ * - There's no instant that has this date/time value in the [timeZone]. Such situation appears when
156→ * the time zone experiences a transition from a lesser to a greater offset. In this case the conversion is performed with
157→ * the lesser offset.

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/TimeZone.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A time zone, provides the conversion between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 * using a collection of rules specifying which [LocalDateTime] value corresponds to each [Instant].
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A time zone, which provides the rules for converting between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * There are two types of time zones:
 * - [FixedOffsetTimeZone]: A time zone where the offset from UTC is always the same.
 * - Region-based time zone: A time zone where the offset from UTC can change over time,
 *   typically due to daylight saving time transitions.
 *
 * ### Acquisition
 * Some ways to acquire a [TimeZone]:
 * - Use [TimeZone.currentSystemDefault] to get the current system time zone.
 * - Use [TimeZone.of] to get a time zone by its ID (e.g., `"Europe/Berlin"` or `"UTC+3"`).
 * - Use [TimeZone.UTC] for the UTC time zone.
 * - Use [FixedOffsetTimeZone] constructor to create a time zone with a specific [UtcOffset].
 *
 * ### Usage
 * [TimeZone] is primarily used to convert between physical [Instant] and civil [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * ```kotlin
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val timeZone = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
 * val localDateTime = instant.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
 *
 * val backToInstant = localDateTime.toInstant(timeZone)
 * ```
 *
 * Note that the conversion from [LocalDateTime] to [Instant] can be ambiguous or invalid due to
 * daylight saving time transitions. This library follows a set of rules to handle such cases
 * (see [LocalDateTime.toInstant]).
 */"}

  6→package kotlinx.datetime
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.UtcOffsetSerializer
 10→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 11→
 12→/**
 13→ * An offset from UTC.
 14→ *
 15→ * Examples of these values:
 16→ * - `Z`, an offset of zero;
 17→ * - `+05`, plus five hours;
 18→ * - `-02`, minus two hours;
 19→ * - `+03:30`, plus three hours and thirty minutes;
 20→ * - `+01:23:45`, plus one hour, 23 minutes, and 45 seconds.
 21→ */
 22→@Serializable(with = UtcOffsetSerializer::class)
 23→public expect class UtcOffset {
 24→    /**
 25→     * The number of seconds from UTC.
 26→     *
 27→     * The larger the value, the earlier some specific civil date/time happens with the offset.
 28→     */
 29→    public val totalSeconds: Int
 30→
 31→    // TODO: Declare and document toString/equals/hashCode
 32→
 33→    public companion object {
 34→        /**
 35→         * The zero offset from UTC, `Z`.
 36→         */
 37→        public val ZERO: UtcOffset
 38→
 39→        /**
 40→         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse].
 41→         *
 42→         * Parses a string that represents a UTC offset and returns the parsed [UtcOffset] value.
 43→         *
 44→         * If [format] is not specified, [Formats.ISO] is used.
 45→         *
 46→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [UtcOffset] are
 47→         * exceeded.
 48→         */
 49→        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset> = getIsoUtcOffestFormat()): UtcOffset
 50→
 51→        /**
 52→         * Creates a new format for parsing and formatting [UtcOffset] values.
 53→         *
 54→         * Example:
 55→         * ```
 56→         * // `GMT` on zero, `+4:30:15`, using a custom format:
 57→         * UtcOffset.Format {
 58→         *   optional("GMT") {
 59→         *     offsetHours(Padding.NONE); char(':'); offsetMinutesOfHour()
 60→         *     optional { char(':'); offsetSecondsOfMinute() }
 61→         *   }
 62→         * }
 63→         * ```
 64→         *
 65→         * Since [UtcOffset] values are rarely formatted and parsed on their own,
 66→         * instances of [DateTimeFormat] obtained here will likely need to be passed to
 67→         * [DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithUtcOffset.offset] in a format builder for a larger data structure.
 68→         *
 69→         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [UtcOffset.Formats].
 70→         *
 71→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 72→         */
 73→        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 74→        public fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithUtcOffset.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
 75→    }
 76→
 77→    /**
 78→     * A collection of predefined formats for parsing and formatting [UtcOffset] values.
 79→     *
 80→     * See [UtcOffset.Formats.ISO], [UtcOffset.Formats.ISO_BASIC], and [UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS]
 81→     * for popular predefined formats.
 82→     *
 83→     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [UtcOffset.Format] to create a custom
 84→     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [UtcOffset] values.
 85→     */
 86→    public object Formats {
 87→        /**
 88→         * ISO 8601 extended format, which is the format used by [UtcOffset.parse] and [UtcOffset.toString].
 89→         *
 90→         * An extension of the ISO 8601 is that this format allows parsing and formatting seconds.
 91→         *
 92→         * When formatting, seconds are omitted if they are zero. If the whole offset is zero, the letter `Z` is output.
 93→         *
 94→         * Examples of UTC offsets in ISO 8601 format:
 95→         * - `Z` or `+00:00`, an offset of zero;
 96→         * - `+05:00`, five hours;
 97→         * - `-02:00`, minus two hours;
 98→         * - `-17:16`
 99→         * - `+10:36:30`
100→         */
101→        public val ISO: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
102→
103→        /**
104→         * ISO 8601 basic format.
105→         *
106→         * An extension of the ISO 8601 is that this format allows parsing and formatting seconds.
107→         *
108→         * When formatting, seconds are omitted if they are zero. If the whole offset is zero, the letter `Z` is output.
109→         *
110→         * Examples of UTC offsets in ISO 8601 basic format:
111→         * - `Z`
112→         * - `+05`
113→         * - `-1716`
114→         * - `+103630`
115→         *
116→         * @see UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS
117→         */
118→        public val ISO_BASIC: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
119→
120→        /**
121→         * A subset of the ISO 8601 basic format that always outputs and parses exactly a numeric sign and four digits:
122→         * two digits for the hour and two digits for the minute. If the offset has a non-zero number of seconds,
123→         * they are truncated.
124→         *
125→         * Examples of UTC offsets in this format:
126→         * - `+0000`
127→         * - `+0500`
128→         * - `-1716`
129→         * - `+1036`
130→         *
131→         * @see UtcOffset.Formats.ISO_BASIC
132→         */
133→        public val FOUR_DIGITS: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
134→    }
135→
136→    /**
137→     * Converts this UTC offset to the extended ISO-8601 string representation.
138→     *
139→     * @see Formats.ISO for the format details.
140→     * @see parse for the dual operation: obtaining [UtcOffset] from a string.
141→     * @see UtcOffset.format for formatting using a custom format.
142→     */
143→    public override fun toString(): String
144→}
145→
146→/**
147→ * Formats this value using the given [format].
148→ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] with `this`.
149→ */
150→public fun UtcOffset.format(format: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>): String = format.format(this)
151→
152→/**
153→ * Constructs a [UtcOffset] from hours, minutes, and seconds components.
154→ *
155→ * All components must have the same sign.
156→ *
157→ * The bounds are checked: it is invalid to pass something other than `±[0; 59]` as the number of seconds or minutes.
158→ * For example, `UtcOffset(hours = 3, minutes = 61)` is invalid.
159→ *
160→ * However, the non-null component of the highest order can exceed these bounds,
161→ * for example, `UtcOffset(minutes = 241)` is valid.
162→ *
163→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if a component exceeds its bounds when a higher order component is specified.
164→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if components have different signs.
165→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the resulting `UtcOffset` value is outside of range `±18:00`.
166→ */
167→public expect fun UtcOffset(hours: Int? = null, minutes: Int? = null, seconds: Int? = null): UtcOffset
168→
169→@Deprecated("Use UtcOffset.ZERO instead", ReplaceWith("UtcOffset.ZERO"), DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
170→public fun UtcOffset(): UtcOffset = UtcOffset.ZERO
171→
172→/**
173→ * Returns the fixed-offset time zone with the given UTC offset.
174→ */
175→public fun UtcOffset.asTimeZone(): FixedOffsetTimeZone = FixedOffsetTimeZone(this)
176→
177→// workaround for https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-65484
178→internal fun getIsoUtcOffestFormat() = UtcOffset.Formats.ISO
179→

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/UtcOffset.kt","old_string":"/**
 * An offset from UTC.
 *
 * Examples of these values:
 * - `Z`, an offset of zero;
 * - `+05`, plus five hours;
 * - `-02`, minus two hours;

 * - `+01:23:45`, plus one hour, 23 minutes, and 45 seconds.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * An offset from UTC, such as `+03:30`.
 *
 * `UtcOffset` represents the amount of time that a local time differs from UTC.
 * It is typically used as part of a [FixedOffsetTimeZone] or [DateTimeComponents].
 *
 * ### Acquisition
 * Some ways to acquire a [UtcOffset]:
 * - From components using the constructor: `UtcOffset(hours = 3, minutes = 30)`.
 * - By parsing an ISO-8601 string: `UtcOffset.parse("+03:30")`.
 * - From an [Instant] and a [TimeZone]: `instant.offsetIn(timeZone)`.
 * - Use [UtcOffset.ZERO] for the UTC offset.
 *
 * ### Usage
 * [UtcOffset] is used to define [FixedOffsetTimeZone] or to represent the offset component in a
 * formatted date-time string.
 *
 * ```kotlin
 * val offset = UtcOffset(hours = 2)
 * val timeZone = offset.asTimeZone()
 * val nowInOffset = Clock.System.now().toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
 * ```
 *
 * The supported range of offsets is from `-18:00` to `+18:00`.
 *
 * ### Interactions with Native Types
 * On the JVM, [UtcOffset] can be converted to and from `java.time.ZoneOffset` using `toJavaZoneOffset()` and
 * `toKotlinUtcOffset()` extension functions.
 */"}

  5→
  6→package kotlinx.datetime
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodIso8601Serializer
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer
 11→import kotlin.math.*
 12→import kotlin.time.Duration
 13→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 14→
 15→/**
 16→ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 17→ *
 18→ * The date components are: [years], [months], [days].
 19→ *
 20→ * The time components are: [hours], [minutes], [seconds], [nanoseconds].
 21→ *

 23→ * [parsed][DateTimePeriod.parse] from a string, or returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 24→ * All these functions can return a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of `DateTimePeriod`,
 25→ * a special case that only stores date components, if all time components of the result happen to be zero.
 26→ */
 27→@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 28→// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 29→public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 30→    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 31→
 32→    /**
 33→     * The number of calendar days.
 34→     *
 35→     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 36→     */
 37→    public abstract val days: Int
 38→    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 39→
 40→    /**
 41→     * The number of whole years.
 42→     */
 43→    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 44→
 45→    /**
 46→     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 47→     */
 48→    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
 49→
 50→    /**
 51→     * The number of whole hours in this period.
 52→     */
 53→    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
 54→
 55→    /**
 56→     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 57→     */
 58→    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
 59→
 60→    /**
 61→     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 62→     */
 63→    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 64→
 65→    /**
 66→     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
 67→     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
 68→     */
 69→    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 70→
 71→    private fun allNonpositive() =
 72→        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
 73→
 74→    /**
 75→     * Converts this period to the ISO-8601 string representation for durations.
 76→     *
 77→     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
 78→     */
 79→    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
 80→        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
 81→        append('P')
 82→        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
 83→        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
 84→        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
 85→        var t = "T"
 86→        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
 87→        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
 88→        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
 89→            append(t)
 90→            append(when {
 91→                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
 92→                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
 93→                else -> "0"
 94→            })
 95→            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
 96→            append('S')
 97→        }
 98→
 99→        if (length == 1) append("0D")
100→    }
101→
102→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
103→        if (this === other) return true
104→        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
105→
106→        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
107→        if (days != other.days) return false
108→        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
109→
110→        return true
111→    }
112→
113→    override fun hashCode(): Int {
114→        var result = totalMonths
115→        result = 31 * result + days
116→        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
117→        return result
118→    }
119→
120→    public companion object {
121→        /**
122→         * Parses a ISO-8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
123→         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
124→         *
125→         * Additionally, we support the `W` signifier to represent weeks.
126→         *
127→         * Examples of durations in the ISO-8601 format:
128→         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
129→         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
130→         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
131→         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
132→         *
133→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
134→         * exceeded.
135→         */
136→        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
137→            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
138→                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
139→            val START = 0
140→            val AFTER_P = 1
141→            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
142→            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
143→            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
144→            val AFTER_DAY = 5
145→            val AFTER_T = 6
146→            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
147→            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
148→            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
149→
150→            var state = START
151→            // next unread character
152→            var i = 0
153→            var sign = 1
154→            var years = 0
155→            var months = 0
156→            var weeks = 0
157→            var days = 0
158→            var hours = 0
159→            var minutes = 0
160→            var seconds = 0
161→            var nanoseconds = 0
162→            while (true) {
163→                if (i >= text.length) {
164→                    if (state == START)
165→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
166→                    if (state == AFTER_T)
167→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
168→                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
169→                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
170→                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
171→                    }
172→                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
173→                }
174→                if (state == START) {
175→                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
176→                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
177→                    when (text[i]) {
178→                        '+', '-' -> {
179→                            if (text[i] == '-')
180→                                sign = -1
181→                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
182→                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
183→                            i += 2
184→                        }
185→                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
186→                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
187→                    }
188→                    state = AFTER_P
189→                    continue
190→                }
191→                var localSign = sign
192→                val iStart = i
193→                when (text[i]) {
194→                    '+', '-' -> {
195→                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
196→                        i += 1
197→                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
198→                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
199→                    }
200→                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
201→                    'T' -> {
202→                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
203→                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
204→                        state = AFTER_T
205→                        i += 1
206→                        continue
207→                    }
208→                }
209→                var number = 0L
210→                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
211→                    try {
212→                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
213→                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
214→                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
215→                    }
216→                    i += 1
217→                }
218→                number *= localSign
219→                if (i == text.length)
220→                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
221→                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
222→                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
223→                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
224→                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
225→                    return toInt()
226→                }
227→                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
228→                    'Y' -> {
229→                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
230→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
231→                        state = AFTER_YEAR
232→                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
233→                    }
234→                    'M' -> {
235→                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
236→                            // Minutes
237→                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
238→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
239→                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
240→                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
241→                        } else {
242→                            // Months
243→                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
244→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
245→                            state = AFTER_MONTH
246→                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
247→                        }
248→                    }
249→                    'W' -> {
250→                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
251→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
252→                        state = AFTER_WEEK
253→                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
254→                    }
255→                    'D' -> {
256→                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
257→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
258→                        state = AFTER_DAY
259→                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
260→                    }
261→                    'H' -> {
262→                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
263→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
264→                        state = AFTER_HOUR
265→                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
266→                    }
267→                    'S' -> {
268→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
269→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
270→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
271→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
272→                    }
273→                    '.', ',' -> {
274→                        i += 1
275→                        if (i >= text.length)
276→                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
277→                        val iStartFraction = i
278→                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')
279→                            i += 1
280→                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
281→                        if (fractionLength > 9)
282→                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
283→                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)
284→                        nanoseconds = fractionalPart.toInt(10) * localSign
285→                        if (text[i] != 'S')
286→                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
287→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
288→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
289→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
290→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
291→                    }
292→                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
293→                }
294→                i += 1
295→           }
296→        }
297→    }
298→}
299→
300→/**
301→ * @suppress
302→ */
303→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DateTimePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
304→public fun String.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = DateTimePeriod.parse(this)
305→
306→/**
307→ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores date components and has all time components equal to zero.
308→ *
309→ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
310→ * the time components are zero.
311→ *
312→ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate],
313→ * but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
314→ */
315→@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
316→public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
317→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
318→    override val days: Int,
319→) : DateTimePeriod() {
320→    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
321→    // avoiding excessive computations
322→    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
323→    override val hours: Int get() = 0
324→
325→    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
326→    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
327→
328→    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
329→    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
330→
331→    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
332→    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
333→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
334→
335→    public companion object {
336→        /**
337→         * Parses the ISO-8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod].
338→         *
339→         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
340→         * zero.
341→         *
342→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
343→         * or any time components are not zero.
344→         *
345→         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
346→         */
347→        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
348→            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
349→                is DatePeriod -> period
350→                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
351→            }
352→    }
353→}
354→
355→/**
356→ * @suppress
357→ */
358→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DatePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
359→public fun String.toDatePeriod(): DatePeriod = DatePeriod.parse(this)
360→
361→private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
362→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
363→    override val days: Int,
364→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
365→) : DateTimePeriod()
366→
367→// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
368→private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
369→    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
370→        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
371→        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
372→    }
373→
374→private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
375→    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
376→    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
377→    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
378→    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
379→    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
380→    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
381→    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
382→    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
383→    return try {
384→        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
385→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
386→        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
387→    }
388→}
389→
390→internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
391→    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
392→        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
393→    else
394→        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
395→
396→/**
397→ * Constructs a new [DateTimePeriod]. If all the time components are zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
398→ *
399→ * It is recommended to always explicitly name the arguments when constructing this manually,
400→ * like `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 12)`.
401→ *
402→ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
403→ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2)`.
404→ *
405→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
406→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [hours], [minutes], [seconds] and [nanoseconds]
407→ * overflows a [Long].
408→ */
409→public fun DateTimePeriod(
410→    years: Int = 0,
411→    months: Int = 0,
412→    days: Int = 0,
413→    hours: Int = 0,
414→    minutes: Int = 0,
415→    seconds: Int = 0,
416→    nanoseconds: Long = 0
417→): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths(years, months), days,
418→    totalNanoseconds(hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds))
419→
420→/**
421→ * Constructs a [DateTimePeriod] from a [Duration].
422→ *
423→ * If the duration value is too big to be represented as a [Long] number of nanoseconds,
424→ * the result will be [Long.MAX_VALUE] nanoseconds.
425→ */
426→// TODO: maybe it's more consistent to throw here on overflow?
427→public fun Duration.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalNanoseconds = inWholeNanoseconds)
428→
429→/**
430→ * Adds two [DateTimePeriod] instances.
431→ *
432→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
433→ */
434→public operator fun DateTimePeriod.plus(other: DateTimePeriod): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(
435→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
436→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
437→    safeAdd(totalNanoseconds, other.totalNanoseconds),
438→)
439→
440→/**
441→ * Adds two [DatePeriod] instances.
442→ *
443→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
444→ */
445→public operator fun DatePeriod.plus(other: DatePeriod): DatePeriod = DatePeriod(
446→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
447→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
448→)

 *
 * `DateTimePeriod` represents a duration in terms of calendar units (years, months, days) and
 * time units (hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds).
 *
 * ### Acquisition
 * Some ways to acquire a [DateTimePeriod]:

 * - By parsing an ISO-8601 duration string: `DateTimePeriod.parse("P1Y6M")`.

 *
 * ### Usage
 * `DateTimePeriod` is used in arithmetic operations on [Instant].
 *

 * val period = DateTimePeriod(months = 1)
 * val nextMonth = Clock.System.now().plus(period, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())

 *
 * A [DatePeriod] is a specialized [DateTimePeriod] that only contains date components.
 *
 * ### Normalization

 * to `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 1)`. However, days are NOT normalized into months because

1→/*

  3→ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4→ */
  5→
  6→package kotlinx.datetime
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.safeMultiply
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.*
 10→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 11→import kotlin.time.*
 12→import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.nanoseconds
 13→
 14→/**
 15→ * A unit for measuring time.
 16→ *
 17→ * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 18→ * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 19→ *
 20→ * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 21→ * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 22→ * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 23→ *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 24→ *
 25→ * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.
 26→ */
 27→@Serializable(with = DateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 28→public sealed class DateTimeUnit {
 29→
 30→    /** Produces a date-time unit that is a multiple of this unit times the specified integer [scalar] value. */
 31→    public abstract operator fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit
 32→
 33→    /**
 34→     * A date-time unit that has the precise time duration.
 35→     *
 36→     * Such units are independent of the time zone.
 37→     * Any such unit can be represented as some fixed number of nanoseconds.
 38→     */
 39→    @Serializable(with = TimeBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 40→    public class TimeBased(
 41→        /**
 42→         * The length of this unit in nanoseconds.
 43→         */
 44→        public val nanoseconds: Long
 45→    ) : DateTimeUnit() {
 46→        private val unitName: String
 47→        private val unitScale: Long
 48→
 49→        init {
 50→            require(nanoseconds > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $nanoseconds ns." }
 51→            // find a concise string representation for the unit with this duration
 52→            when {
 53→                nanoseconds % 3600_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 54→                    unitName = "HOUR"
 55→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 3600_000_000_000
 56→                }
 57→                nanoseconds % 60_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 58→                    unitName = "MINUTE"
 59→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 60_000_000_000
 60→                }
 61→                nanoseconds % 1_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 62→                    unitName = "SECOND"
 63→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000_000
 64→                }
 65→                nanoseconds % 1_000_000 == 0L -> {
 66→                    unitName = "MILLISECOND"
 67→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000
 68→                }
 69→                nanoseconds % 1_000 == 0L -> {
 70→                    unitName = "MICROSECOND"
 71→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000
 72→                }
 73→                else -> {
 74→                    unitName = "NANOSECOND"
 75→                    unitScale = nanoseconds
 76→                }
 77→            }
 78→        }
 79→
 80→        override fun times(scalar: Int): TimeBased = TimeBased(safeMultiply(nanoseconds, scalar.toLong()))
 81→
 82→        /**
 83→         * The length of this unit as a [Duration].
 84→         */
 85→        public val duration: Duration
 86→            get() = nanoseconds.nanoseconds
 87→
 88→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 89→            this === other || (other is TimeBased && this.nanoseconds == other.nanoseconds)
 90→
 91→        override fun hashCode(): Int = nanoseconds.toInt() xor (nanoseconds shr Int.SIZE_BITS).toInt()
 92→
 93→        override fun toString(): String = formatToString(unitScale, unitName)
 94→    }
 95→
 96→    /**
 97→     * A date-time unit equal to some number of days or months.
 98→     *
 99→     * Operations involving `DateBased` units are performed on dates. The same operations on [Instants][Instant]
100→     * require a [TimeZone] to find the corresponding [LocalDateTimes][LocalDateTime] first to perform
101→     * the operation with the date component of these `LocalDateTime` values.
102→     */
103→    @Serializable(with = DateBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
104→    public sealed class DateBased : DateTimeUnit() {
105→        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
106→        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.DayBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.DayBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
107→        public typealias DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased
108→        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
109→        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
110→        public typealias MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased
111→    }
112→
113→    /**
114→     * A date-time unit equal to some number of calendar days.
115→     *
116→     * A calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours, thus a `DayBased`-unit cannot be expressed as a multiple of some [TimeBased]-unit.
117→     *
118→     * The reason lies in time zone transitions, because of which some days can be 23 or 25 hours.
119→     * For example, we say that exactly a whole day has passed between `2019-10-27T02:59` and `2019-10-28T02:59`
120→     * in Berlin, despite the fact that the clocks were turned back one hour, so there are, in fact, 25 hours
121→     * between the two date-times.
122→     */
123→    @Serializable(with = DayBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
124→    public class DayBased(
125→        /**
126→         * The length of this unit in days.
127→         */
128→        public val days: Int
129→    ) : DateBased() {
130→        init {
131→            require(days > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $days days." }
132→        }
133→
134→        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased(safeMultiply(days, scalar))
135→
136→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
137→            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.DayBased && this.days == other.days)
138→
139→        override fun hashCode(): Int = days xor 0x10000
140→
141→        override fun toString(): String = if (days % 7 == 0)
142→            formatToString(days / 7, "WEEK")
143→        else
144→            formatToString(days, "DAY")
145→    }
146→
147→    /**
148→     * A date-time unit equal to some number of months.
149→     *
150→     * Since different months have different number of days, a `MonthBased`-unit cannot be expressed a multiple of some [DayBased]-unit.
151→     */
152→    @Serializable(with = MonthBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
153→    public class MonthBased(
154→        /**
155→         * The length of this unit in months.
156→         */
157→        public val months: Int
158→    ) : DateBased() {
159→        init {
160→            require(months > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $months months." }
161→        }
162→
163→        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased(safeMultiply(months, scalar))
164→
165→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
166→            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased && this.months == other.months)
167→
168→        override fun hashCode(): Int = months xor 0x20000
169→
170→        override fun toString(): String = when {
171→            months % 12_00 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12_00, "CENTURY")
172→            months % 12 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12, "YEAR")
173→            months % 3 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 3, "QUARTER")
174→            else -> formatToString(months, "MONTH")
175→        }
176→    }
177→
178→    protected fun formatToString(value: Int, unit: String): String = if (value == 1) unit else "$value-$unit"
179→    protected fun formatToString(value: Long, unit: String): String = if (value == 1L) unit else "$value-$unit"
180→
181→    public companion object {
182→        /**
183→         * A nanosecond, which is `1/1_000_000_000` of a second.
184→         */
185→        public val NANOSECOND: TimeBased = TimeBased(nanoseconds = 1)
186→
187→        /**
188→         * A microsecond, which is `1/1_000_000` of a second, or `1_000` nanoseconds.
189→         */
190→        public val MICROSECOND: TimeBased = NANOSECOND * 1000
191→
192→        /**
193→         * A millisecond, which is `1/1_000` of a second, or `1_000_000` nanoseconds.
194→         */
195→        public val MILLISECOND: TimeBased = MICROSECOND * 1000
196→
197→        /**
198→         * A second.
199→         */
200→        public val SECOND: TimeBased = MILLISECOND * 1000
201→
202→        /**
203→         * A minute, which is 60 seconds.
204→         */
205→        public val MINUTE: TimeBased = SECOND * 60
206→
207→        /**
208→         * An hour, which is 60 minutes, or 3600 seconds.
209→         */
210→        public val HOUR: TimeBased = MINUTE * 60
211→
212→        /**
213→         * A calendar day.
214→         *
215→         * Note that a calendar day is not the same as 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
216→         */
217→        public val DAY: DayBased = DayBased(days = 1)
218→
219→        /**
220→         * A week, which is 7 [calendar days][DAY].
221→         */
222→        public val WEEK: DayBased = DAY * 7
223→
224→        /**
225→         * A month.
226→         *
227→         * Note that a month doesn't have a constant number of calendar days in it.
228→         */
229→        public val MONTH: MonthBased = MonthBased(months = 1)
230→
231→        /**
232→         * A quarter, which is three [months][MONTH].
233→         */
234→        public val QUARTER: MonthBased = MONTH * 3
235→
236→        /**
237→         * A year, which is 12 [months][MONTH].
238→         */
239→        public val YEAR: MonthBased = MONTH * 12
240→
241→        /**
242→         * A century, which is 100 [years][YEAR], or 1200 [months][MONTH].
243→         */
244→        public val CENTURY: MonthBased = YEAR * 100
245→    }
246→}

 * A unit for measuring time.
 *
 * There are three types of [DateTimeUnit]:
 * - [TimeBased]: Units with a precise duration that can be expressed in nanoseconds (e.g., hours, seconds).
 * - [DayBased]: Units based on a number of calendar days (e.g., days, weeks).
 * - [MonthBased]: Units based on a number of months (e.g., months, years).
 *
 * ### Acquisition
 * Use the predefined constants in the companion object for common units:
 * - [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.HOUR], [DateTimeUnit.DAY], [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], [DateTimeUnit.YEAR], etc.
 *
 * You can also create custom units by multiplying existing ones:
 * ```kotlin
 * val TEN_MINUTES = DateTimeUnit.MINUTE * 10
 * ```
 *
 * ### Usage
 * [DateTimeUnit] is used in arithmetic operations on [Instant] and [LocalDate].
 *
 * ```kotlin
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val tomorrow = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 *
 * val date = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * val nextYear = date.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.YEAR)
 * ```
 *
 * Note that a calendar day is not always 24 hours due to daylight saving time transitions.
 * Use [TimeBased] units when you need a precise duration, and [DateBased] units when you want
 * calendar-relative arithmetic.
 */","file_path":"/project/core/common/src/DateTimeUnit.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A unit for measuring time.
 *
 * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 *
 * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 *
 * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.

1→/*
  2→ * Copyright 2019-2023 JetBrains s.r.o. and contributors.
  3→ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.

  5→
  6→package kotlinx.datetime.format
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.format.*
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.format.parser.*
 11→
 12→/**
 13→ * A format for parsing and formatting date-time-related values.
 14→ */
 15→public sealed interface DateTimeFormat<T> {
 16→    /**
 17→     * Formats the given [value] into a string, using this format.
 18→     */
 19→    public fun format(value: T): String
 20→
 21→    /**
 22→     * Formats the given [value] into the given [appendable] using this format.
 23→     */
 24→    public fun <A : Appendable> formatTo(appendable: A, value: T): A
 25→
 26→    /**
 27→     * Parses the given [input] string as [T] using this format.
 28→     *
 29→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the input string is not in the expected format or the value is invalid.
 30→     */
 31→    public fun parse(input: CharSequence): T
 32→
 33→    /**
 34→     * Parses the given [input] string as [T] using this format.
 35→     *
 36→     * @return the parsed value, or `null` if the input string is not in the expected format or the value is invalid.
 37→     */
 38→    public fun parseOrNull(input: CharSequence): T?
 39→
 40→    public companion object {
 41→        /**
 42→         * Produces Kotlin code that, when pasted into a Kotlin source file, creates a [DateTimeFormat] instance that
 43→         * behaves identically to [format].
 44→         *
 45→         * The typical use case for this is to create a [DateTimeFormat] instance using a non-idiomatic approach and
 46→         * then convert it to a builder DSL.
 47→         */
 48→        public fun formatAsKotlinBuilderDsl(format: DateTimeFormat<*>): String = when (format) {
 49→            is AbstractDateTimeFormat<*, *> -> format.actualFormat.builderString(allFormatConstants)
 50→        }
 51→    }
 52→}
 53→
 54→/**
 55→ * The style of padding to use when formatting a value.
 56→ */
 57→public enum class Padding {
 58→    /**
 59→     * No padding.
 60→     */
 61→    NONE,
 62→
 63→    /**
 64→     * Pad with zeros.
 65→     */
 66→    ZERO,
 67→
 68→    /**
 69→     * Pad with spaces.
 70→     */
 71→    SPACE
 72→}
 73→
 74→internal fun Padding.toKotlinCode(): String = when (this) {
 75→    Padding.NONE -> "Padding.NONE"
 76→    Padding.ZERO -> "Padding.ZERO"
 77→    Padding.SPACE -> "Padding.SPACE"
 78→}
 79→
 80→internal inline fun Padding.minDigits(width: Int) = if (this == Padding.ZERO) width else 1
 81→internal inline fun Padding.spaces(width: Int) = if (this == Padding.SPACE) width else null
 82→
 83→/** [T] is the user-visible type, whereas [U] is its mutable representation for parsing and formatting. */
 84→internal sealed class AbstractDateTimeFormat<T, U : Copyable<U>> : DateTimeFormat<T> {
 85→
 86→    abstract val actualFormat: CachedFormatStructure<U>
 87→
 88→    abstract fun intermediateFromValue(value: T): U
 89→
 90→    abstract fun valueFromIntermediate(intermediate: U): T
 91→
 92→    abstract val emptyIntermediate: U // should be part of the `Copyable` interface once the language allows this
 93→
 94→    open fun valueFromIntermediateOrNull(intermediate: U): T? = try {
 95→        valueFromIntermediate(intermediate)
 96→    } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
 97→        null
 98→    }
 99→
100→    override fun format(value: T): String = StringBuilder().also {
101→        actualFormat.formatter().format(intermediateFromValue(value), it)
102→    }.toString()
103→
104→    override fun <A : Appendable> formatTo(appendable: A, value: T): A = appendable.apply {
105→        actualFormat.formatter().format(intermediateFromValue(value), this)
106→    }
107→
108→    override fun parse(input: CharSequence): T {
109→        val matched = try {
110→            // without the fully qualified name, the compilation fails for some reason
111→            Parser(actualFormat.parser()).match(input, emptyIntermediate)
112→        } catch (e: ParseException) {
113→            throw DateTimeFormatException("Failed to parse value from '$input'", e)
114→        }
115→        try {
116→            return valueFromIntermediate(matched)
117→        } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
118→            throw DateTimeFormatException(when (val message = e.message) {
119→                null -> "The value parsed from '$input' is invalid"
120→                else -> "$message (when parsing '$input')"
121→            }, e)
122→        }
123→    }
124→
125→    override fun parseOrNull(input: CharSequence): T? =
126→        // without the fully qualified name, the compilation fails for some reason
127→        Parser(actualFormat.parser()).matchOrNull(input, emptyIntermediate)?.let { valueFromIntermediateOrNull(it) }
128→
129→}
130→
131→private val allFormatConstants: List<Pair<String, CachedFormatStructure<*>>> by lazy {
132→    fun unwrap(format: DateTimeFormat<*>): CachedFormatStructure<*> = (format as AbstractDateTimeFormat<*, *>).actualFormat
133→    // the formats are ordered vaguely by decreasing length, as the topmost among suitable ones is chosen.
134→    listOf(
135→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents::dateTimeComponents.name}(DateTimeComponents.Formats.RFC_1123)" to
136→            unwrap(DateTimeComponents.Formats.RFC_1123),
137→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents::dateTimeComponents.name}(DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET)" to
138→            unwrap(DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET),
139→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTime::date.name}(LocalDateTime.Formats.ISO)" to unwrap(LocalDateTime.Formats.ISO),
140→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDate::date.name}(LocalDate.Formats.ISO)" to unwrap(LocalDate.Formats.ISO),
141→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDate::date.name}(LocalDate.Formats.ISO_BASIC)" to unwrap(LocalDate.Formats.ISO_BASIC),
142→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithTime::time.name}(LocalTime.Formats.ISO)" to unwrap(LocalTime.Formats.ISO),
143→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithUtcOffset::offset.name}(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO)" to unwrap(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO),
144→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithUtcOffset::offset.name}(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO_BASIC)" to unwrap(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO_BASIC),
145→        "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithUtcOffset::offset.name}(UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS)" to unwrap(UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS),
146→    )
147→}
148→

 * A format for parsing and formatting date-time-related values.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A format for parsing and formatting date-time-related values.
 *
 * `DateTimeFormat` allows converting between a string representation and a date-time object.
 *
 * ### Acquisition
 * Predefined formats are available in the `Formats` companion objects of date-time classes,
 * for example [LocalDate.Formats.ISO].
 *
 * Custom formats can be created using the `Format` function on the respective class:
 * ```kotlin
 * val myFormat = LocalDate.Format {
 *     year()
 *     char('-')
 *     monthNumber()
 *     char('-')
 *     dayOfMonth()
 * }
 * ```
 *
 * ### Usage
 * Use [format] to convert a value to a string and [parse] to convert a string to a value.
 *
 * ```kotlin
 * val date = LocalDate(2023, 12, 24)
 * val formatted = myFormat.format(date) // "2023-12-24"
 * val parsed = myFormat.parse(formatted)
 * ```
 *
 * @param T the type of the value to be formatted and parsed.
 */","file_path":"/project/core/common/src/format/DateTimeFormat.kt"}

1→/*
  2→ * Copyright 2019-2023 JetBrains s.r.o. and contributors.
  3→ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4→ */
  5→
  6→package kotlinx.datetime.format
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.DayOfWeek
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 11→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.format.*
 12→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.format.parser.Copyable
 13→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.safeMultiply
 14→import kotlin.reflect.*
 15→
 16→/**
 17→ * A collection of date-time fields, used specifically for parsing and formatting.
 18→ *
 19→ * Its main purpose is to provide support for complex date-time formats that don't correspond to any of the standard
 20→ * entities in the library. For example, a format that includes only the month and the day of the month, but not the
 21→ * year, can not be represented and parsed as a [LocalDate], but it is valid for a [DateTimeComponents].
 22→ *
 23→ * Example:
 24→ * ```
 25→ * val input = "2020-03-16T23:59:59.999999999+03:00"
 26→ * val components = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET.parse(input)
 27→ * val localDateTime = components.toLocalDateTime() // LocalDateTime(2020, 3, 16, 23, 59, 59, 999_999_999)
 28→ * val instant = components.toInstantUsingOffset() // Instant.parse("2020-03-16T20:59:59.999999999Z")
 29→ * val offset = components.toUtcOffset() // UtcOffset(hours = 3)
 30→ * ```
 31→ *
 32→ * Another purpose is to support parsing and formatting data with out-of-bounds values. For example, parsing
 33→ * `23:59:60` as a [LocalTime] is not possible, but it is possible to parse it as a [DateTimeComponents], adjust the value by
 34→ * setting [second] to `59`, and then convert it to a [LocalTime] via [toLocalTime].
 35→ *
 36→ * Example:
 37→ * ```
 38→ * val input = "23:59:60"
 39→ * val extraDay: Boolean
 40→ * val time = DateTimeComponents.Format {
 41→ *   time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO)
 42→ * }.parse(input).apply {
 43→ *   if (hour == 23 && minute == 59 && second == 60) {
 44→ *     hour = 0; minute = 0; second = 0; extraDay = true
 45→ *   } else {
 46→ *     extraDay = false
 47→ *   }
 48→ * }.toLocalTime()
 49→ * ```
 50→ *
 51→ * Because this class has limited applications, constructing it directly is not possible.
 52→ * For formatting, use the [format] overload that accepts a lambda with a [DateTimeComponents] receiver.
 53→ *
 54→ * Example:
 55→ * ```
 56→ * // Mon, 16 Mar 2020 23:59:59 +0300
 57→ * DateTimeComponents.Formats.RFC_1123.format {
 58→ *    setDateTimeOffset(LocalDateTime(2020, 3, 16, 23, 59, 59, 999_999_999))
 59→ *    setDateTimeOffset(UtcOffset(hours = 3))
 60→ * }
 61→ * ```
 62→ *
 63→ * Accessing the fields of this class is not thread-safe.
 64→ * Make sure to apply proper synchronization if you are using a single instance from multiple threads.
 65→ */
 66→public class DateTimeComponents internal constructor(internal val contents: DateTimeComponentsContents = DateTimeComponentsContents()) {
 67→    public companion object {
 68→        /**
 69→         * Creates a [DateTimeFormat] for [DateTimeComponents] values using [DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents].
 70→         *
 71→         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [DateTimeComponents.Formats].
 72→         *
 73→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 74→         */
 75→        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 76→        public fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> {
 77→            val builder = DateTimeComponentsFormat.Builder(AppendableFormatStructure())
 78→            block(builder)
 79→            return DateTimeComponentsFormat(builder.build())
 80→        }
 81→    }
 82→
 83→    /**
 84→     * A collection of formats for parsing and formatting [DateTimeComponents] values.
 85→     *
 86→     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [DateTimeComponents.Format] to create a custom
 87→     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [DateTimeComponents] values.
 88→     */
 89→    public object Formats {
 90→
 91→        /**
 92→         * ISO 8601 extended format for dates and times with UTC offset.
 93→         *
 94→         * For specifying the time zone offset, the format uses the [UtcOffset.Formats.ISO] format, except that during
 95→         * parsing, specifying the minutes is optional.
 96→         *
 97→         * This format differs from [LocalTime.Formats.ISO] in its time part in that
 98→         * specifying the seconds is *not* optional.
 99→         *
100→         * Examples of instants in the ISO 8601 format:
101→         * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00Z`
102→         * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00.50Z`
103→         * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00.123456789Z`
104→         * - `2020-08-30T18:40:00+03:00`
105→         * - `2020-08-30T18:40:00+03:30:20`
106→         * * `2020-01-01T23:59:59.123456789+01`
107→         * * `+12020-01-31T23:59:59Z`
108→         *
109→         * This format uses the local date, local time, and UTC offset fields of [DateTimeComponents].
110→         *
111→         * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.4.2.1b), excluding the format without the offset.
112→         *
113→         * Guaranteed to parse all strings that [Instant.toString] produces.
114→         */
115→        public val ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = Format {
116→            date(ISO_DATE)
117→            alternativeParsing({
118→                char('t')
119→            }) {
120→                char('T')
121→            }
122→            hour()
123→            char(':')
124→            minute()
125→            char(':')
126→            second()
127→            optional {
128→                char('.')
129→                secondFraction(1, 9)
130→            }
131→            alternativeParsing({
132→                offsetHours()
133→            }) {
134→                offset(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO)
135→            }
136→        }
137→
138→        /**
139→         * RFC 1123 format for dates and times with UTC offset.
140→         *
141→         * Examples of valid strings:
142→         * * `Mon, 30 Jun 2008 11:05:30 GMT`
143→         * * `Mon, 30 Jun 2008 11:05:30 -0300`
144→         * * `30 Jun 2008 11:05:30 UT`
145→         *
146→         * North American and military time zone abbreviations are not supported.
147→         */
148→        public val RFC_1123: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = Format {
149→            alternativeParsing({
150→                // the day of week may be missing
151→            }) {
152→                dayOfWeek(DayOfWeekNames.ENGLISH_ABBREVIATED)
153→                chars(", ")
154→            }
155→            dayOfMonth(Padding.NONE)
156→            char(' ')
157→            monthName(MonthNames.ENGLISH_ABBREVIATED)
158→            char(' ')
159→            year()
160→            char(' ')
161→            hour()
162→            char(':')
163→            minute()
164→            optional {
165→                char(':')
166→                second()

495→internal class DateTimeComponentsContents internal constructor(
496→    val date: IncompleteLocalDate = IncompleteLocalDate(),
497→    val time: IncompleteLocalTime = IncompleteLocalTime(),
498→    val offset: IncompleteUtcOffset = IncompleteUtcOffset(),
499→    var timeZoneId: String? = null,
500→) : DateFieldContainer by date, TimeFieldContainer by time, UtcOffsetFieldContainer by offset,
501→    DateTimeFieldContainer, Copyable<DateTimeComponentsContents> {
502→    override fun copy(): DateTimeComponentsContents =
503→        DateTimeComponentsContents(date.copy(), time.copy(), offset.copy(), timeZoneId)
504→
505→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
506→        other is DateTimeComponentsContents && other.date == date && other.time == time &&
507→            other.offset == offset && other.timeZoneId == timeZoneId
508→
509→    override fun hashCode(): Int =
510→        date.hashCode() xor time.hashCode() xor offset.hashCode() xor (timeZoneId?.hashCode() ?: 0)
511→}
512→
513→internal val timeZoneField = GenericFieldSpec(PropertyAccessor(DateTimeComponentsContents::timeZoneId))
514→
515→internal class TimeZoneIdDirective(private val knownZones: Set<String>) :
516→    StringFieldFormatDirective<DateTimeComponentsContents>(timeZoneField, knownZones) {
517→
518→    override val builderRepresentation: String
519→        get() =
520→            "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents::timeZoneId.name}()"
521→
522→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean = other is TimeZoneIdDirective && other.knownZones == knownZones
523→    override fun hashCode(): Int = knownZones.hashCode()
524→}

526→internal class DateTimeComponentsFormat(override val actualFormat: CachedFormatStructure<DateTimeComponentsContents>) :
527→    AbstractDateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents, DateTimeComponentsContents>() {
528→    override fun intermediateFromValue(value: DateTimeComponents): DateTimeComponentsContents = value.contents
529→
530→    override fun valueFromIntermediate(intermediate: DateTimeComponentsContents): DateTimeComponents =
531→        DateTimeComponents(intermediate)
532→
533→    override val emptyIntermediate get() = emptyDateTimeComponentsContents
534→
535→    class Builder(override val actualBuilder: AppendableFormatStructure<DateTimeComponentsContents>) :
536→        AbstractDateTimeFormatBuilder<DateTimeComponentsContents, Builder>, AbstractWithDateTimeBuilder,
537→        AbstractWithOffsetBuilder, DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents {
538→        override fun addFormatStructureForDateTime(structure: FormatStructure<DateTimeFieldContainer>) {
539→            actualBuilder.add(structure)
540→        }
541→
542→        override fun addFormatStructureForOffset(structure: FormatStructure<UtcOffsetFieldContainer>) {
543→            actualBuilder.add(structure)
544→        }
545→
546→        override fun timeZoneId() =
547→            actualBuilder.add(BasicFormatStructure(TimeZoneIdDirective(TimeZone.availableZoneIds)))
548→
549→        @Suppress("NO_ELSE_IN_WHEN")
550→        override fun dateTimeComponents(format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>) = when (format) {
551→            is DateTimeComponentsFormat -> actualBuilder.add(format.actualFormat)
552→        }
553→
554→        override fun createEmpty(): Builder = Builder(AppendableFormatStructure())

558→private class TwoDigitNumber(private val reference: KMutableProperty0<Int?>) {
559→    operator fun getValue(thisRef: Any?, property: KProperty<*>) = reference.getValue(thisRef, property)
560→
561→    operator fun setValue(thisRef: Any?, property: KProperty<*>, value: Int?) {
562→        require(value === null || value in 0..99) { "${property.name} must be a two-digit number, got '$value'" }
